title: Adult Literacy Circle
description: Free English reading and writing practice. Materials provided. Drop-in, no registration.

title: Celebrate Hmong New Year with HAP, March 8
description:
Hmong American Partnership invites the whole community to its annual Hmong New Year Celebration on Saturday, March 8 at the HAP Community Center, 1075 Arcade St., St. Paul.

Doors open at 11am. Program begins at noon with traditional pov pob (ball toss), cultural performances, and music. Traditional Hmong food will be served; admission includes a meal. Children's activities run all afternoon.

Free and open to all. No registration required. Parking available in the Arcade St. lot. Bus line 61 stops two blocks north.
summary: HAP's Hmong New Year Celebration is March 8 at 1075 Arcade St. Doors open 11am. Traditional food, performances, children's games. Free and open to all.

title: Citizenship Test Prep Class
description: Practice civics questions and English skills for the naturalization interview. Interpreters available.

title: Community Food Drive & Resource Fair
description: Bring non-perishable items or come browse tables from 15+ local service organizations. Free lunch for all.

title: Community Health Fair & Free Screenings
description: Blood pressure, glucose, vision, and dental screenings. Flu shots available. Interpretation in 6 languages.

title: Craft & Connection Morning at Community Thread
description: Join Community Thread in Stillwater for a drop-in morning of knitting, crafting, and conversation. All skill levels welcome. Supplies provided. Coffee and tea available. Great for anyone feeling isolated or just looking for company on a weekday morning. No registration needed.
summary: Drop-in crafting and conversation at Community Thread every Wednesday morning. All skill levels, supplies included, no registration required.

title: Drop Off Donations at Lakeville's Spring Food Drive
description:
The 360 Communities Messiah Community Food Shelf at Messiah Lutheran Church (17305 Kenwood Trail, Lakeville) is hosting its annual spring food drive on Saturday, March 14, 10am–2pm.

Drop off non-perishable items or make a financial donation. $10 buys five meals. Most needed items: canned protein (tuna, chicken, beans), peanut butter, pasta, and cooking oil. Families with children's sizes clothing in good condition can bring those too; they'll be passed along to families in need.

Volunteers on-site to unload and sort. The food shelf serves 300+ households each month. For questions call 952-985-5300.
summary: Bring non-perishables to Messiah Lutheran in Lakeville on March 14, 10am–2pm. The 360 Communities food shelf serves 300+ households monthly.

title: Free Benefits Help at Eagan Walk-in Clinic, First Tuesdays
description:
Dakota County's Economic Assistance office hosts a monthly walk-in clinic for residents who need help applying for or managing public benefits including SNAP, Medical Assistance, cash assistance, and energy assistance.

First Tuesday of each month, 9am–noon at the Northern Service Center, 1 Mendota Rd W, West St. Paul (use the main entrance and ask for the Benefits Help Clinic at the front desk).

No appointment needed. Bring: photo ID, proof of address, recent pay stubs or benefits letters, and social security numbers for all household members. Interpreters available; call 651-554-6100 at least 48 hours ahead to request a language.
summary: Dakota County runs a free benefits walk-in clinic on the first Tuesday of each month in West St. Paul. Covers SNAP, Medical Assistance, and more.

title: Free Benefits Screening at Jordan Library, March 12
description:
Scott County Human Services is hosting a free benefits screening clinic at the Jordan Public Library on Thursday, March 12, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m.

County navigators will help residents check eligibility for food support (SNAP), heating assistance (LIHEAP), health coverage (Medical Assistance), and emergency cash programs. Bring ID and a recent piece of mail showing your address. Interpreters available for Spanish.

No appointment needed. The clinic is open to all Scott County residents. This is a screening; navigators can begin applications on-site and follow up by phone.
summary: Scott County navigators at Jordan Library on March 12, 10 a.m.–2 p.m. Free screening for SNAP, heating aid, health coverage, and emergency cash. Spanish interpretation available.

title: Get Help Filing Your Citizenship Application, March 15
description:
The International Institute of Minnesota is hosting a free citizenship application assistance workshop on Saturday, March 15, 9am–3pm, at 1694 Como Ave., St. Paul.

Staff and volunteer attorneys will help you complete the N-400 form, review your documents, and answer questions about the naturalization process. Interpretation available in Spanish, Somali, Hmong, Vietnamese, and Karen.

What to bring: your green card, a government-issued photo ID, any previous immigration documents, and two passport photos.

Space is limited to 40 participants. Register by March 11 at iimn.org or call 651-647-0191.
summary: International Institute is offering free citizenship application help on March 15. Bring your green card and two passport photos. Register by March 11.

title: Karen New Year Celebration
description: Cultural performances, traditional food, and family activities. All are welcome.

title: Know Your Rights Workshop
description: Tenant rights, immigration basics, and workplace protections. Childcare provided. RSVP at ilcm.org.

title: Morning Recovery Meeting
description: Open meeting. Coffee provided. All are welcome.

title: Pick Up Groceries at New Prague's March Mobile Pantry
description:
The Peace Center Food Shelf is hosting its monthly mobile pantry distribution on Saturday, March 7. Pickup runs from 9 a.m. to noon at the Peace Center parking lot on Main Street.

No registration required. Families receive a full bag of groceries including fresh produce, proteins, and shelf-stable items. Quantities are limited, so arrive early for the best selection.

The Peace Center also operates a walk-in pantry Tuesday and Thursday mornings. The mobile event is an additional distribution for neighbors who can't make weekday hours.
summary: Peace Center's March mobile pantry runs Saturday, March 7, 9 a.m.–noon in New Prague. No registration. Fresh produce, protein, and shelf-stable items while supplies last.

title: Spring Resource Fair: Washington County Orgs Together
description: Valley Outreach hosts its annual spring resource fair at the Stillwater Public Library community room. Representatives from Basic Needs Inc., Community Thread, Open Cupboard, Friends In Need, Washington County Human Services, and 10 other organizations will be on hand. Learn what help is available, ask questions, and connect with neighbors. Free. No ID required.
summary: Valley Outreach brings together 15+ Washington County organizations at the Stillwater Public Library. Free, no ID required. Come find out what help is available.

title: Tenant Rights Clinic
description: Free virtual clinic. One-on-one with housing attorneys. Spanish interpretation available.

title: Union Gospel Mission Job Fair: 20+ Employers, March 22
description:
Union Gospel Mission Twin Cities is hosting a community job fair on Saturday, March 22, 10am–2pm at 435 University Ave. E., St. Paul. More than 20 employers will be on-site, including manufacturing, food service, healthcare support, and warehouse positions.

Many employers on-site are background-check friendly and offer on-the-job training. You do not need a resume to attend, but bringing one helps. Dress professionally if you can.

UGM staff will be available for quick resume reviews before the fair opens (9–10am). Childcare is available on-site, so bring your kids.

Free. No registration required. Open to all.
summary: UGM is hosting a job fair on March 22 with 20+ employers, including background-check-friendly companies. Free. No registration. Childcare available.

title: Women's Health Workshop at Isuroon, Free, Saturday March 21
description:
Isuroon is hosting a free women's health workshop at their Burnsville office (300 E Travelers Trail, Suite 210) on Saturday, March 21, 10am–1pm.

Topics covered: preventive screenings, maternal health, navigating the health care system without insurance, and how to access low-cost dental and vision care. A Somali-speaking nurse practitioner will present. Lunch provided.

Open to all women in the south metro. No registration required. Walk in. For more information call 952-303-3489.
summary: Free women's health workshop at Isuroon in Burnsville on March 21, 10am–1pm. Somali-speaking nurse on-site. Lunch included. Walk-ins welcome.

title: ESL Conversation Partners
description: Practice English with adult learners, 1 hr/week. Daytime and evening slots. No teaching experience needed.

title: Family of 4 Needs Temporary Housing in South Metro
description: Parents with two children (ages 3 and 7) currently in shelter. Employed, looking for 2BR in Bloomington/Richfield/Mpls area. Can pay up to $1,200/mo. Case manager available.

title: Food Shelf Needs Saturday Volunteers Starting March 7
description:
Merrick Community Services is opening Saturday hours at their food shelf at 2700 E. Minnehaha Ave., St. Paul, and needs volunteers to make it happen. Shifts run 8:30am–1pm. Tasks include unloading donations, stocking shelves, greeting clients, and helping with intake.

No experience required; you will be trained. Must be 16 or older. Background check required for anyone working with client data.

To sign up: call 651-348-8530 or email volunteer@merrickcs.org. Bilingual volunteers (English/Spanish, English/Somali) especially welcome.
summary: Merrick Community Services needs Saturday volunteers to staff new weekend food shelf hours. Shifts 8:30am–1pm starting March 7. Call 651-348-8530.

title: Food Shelf Volunteers, Weekday Mornings
description: Sort and stock donations, greet clients, help with intake paperwork. Tuesday through Friday, 8:30am–12pm. No experience needed. Background check required.

title: Food Shelves Need More Hands as Demand Surges
description:
Client visits at CEAP's food shelf in Brooklyn Center have climbed steadily for three years running, and the volunteer crew that sorts, stocks, and distributes is stretched thin. They need people who can commit to a regular weekly or biweekly shift: sorting donated food, restocking shelves, and helping families navigate the client-choice shopping floor.

Shifts run Monday through Friday during food shelf hours. No experience required. You should be able to stand for a few hours and lift grocery-weight boxes. If you can commit to the same slot each week, even better, because consistency matters when the team is small. Contact CEAP's volunteer coordinator at **763-566-9600** or stop by 6840 78th Ave N, Brooklyn Center, and ask for the volunteer desk.
summary: CEAP's Brooklyn Center food shelf needs weekly volunteers to sort, stock, and help families shop. Call 763-566-9600 to sign up.

title: Free After-School Program for Ages 6–12
description: Homework help, arts, and recreation Mon–Fri 3–6pm. Snack included. Registration open for spring semester.

title: Free Furniture: Beds, Tables, Chairs
description: Gently used furniture for individuals and families setting up new homes. By referral from case manager.

title: Free Grocery Bags Every Wednesday, No Questions Asked
description:
Open Hands Midway distributes free grocery bags every Wednesday evening, 5–7pm, at 1961 W. University Ave., St. Paul (enter from Fry St.). No ID, no income verification, no appointment.

Bags include fresh produce, proteins, dairy, and shelf-stable items. Dietary options available: halal and vegetarian bags can be requested at the door.

Families, individuals, and neighbors picking up for someone else are all welcome. Parking available in the lot behind the building. Bus lines 16 and 50 stop one block away.
summary: Open Hands Midway gives away free grocery bags every Wednesday, 5–7pm at 1961 W. University Ave. No ID, no forms. Halal and vegetarian options available.

title: Free Produce: Pick Up at Apple Valley Site
description:
The Open Door Pantry has surplus fresh produce available at their Apple Valley mobile distribution site this Thursday and Friday, 3–6pm. Location: 14200 Cedar Ave, Apple Valley (east parking lot). No appointment needed. No ID required.

Available while supplies last: apples, sweet potatoes, cabbage, and canned goods. Quantities are first-come, first-served. Spanish and Somali speakers available on-site Thursday.

The Open Door Pantry operates multiple Dakota County sites. For a full schedule visit theopendoorpantry.org or call 952-431-3140.
summary: The Open Door Pantry has surplus produce at their Apple Valley site Thursday and Friday, 3–6pm. No ID, no appointment. First come, first served.

title: Free Rides to Medical Appointments
description: Door-to-door service for eligible residents. Schedule 1–3 days ahead. Wheelchair accessible vehicles available.

title: Hastings Food Market Needs Weekend Volunteers
description:
Hastings Family Service runs a client-choice food market model at 700 W. 3rd St where neighbors shop for groceries with dignity, not pre-packed boxes. They need 4–6 volunteers each Saturday from 8:30am to 12:30pm to restock shelves, assist shoppers, and help with checkout.

No experience required. Volunteers must be at least 16 years old. Background check required for anyone working with children's programming. To sign up, email volunteer@hastingsfamilyservice.org or call 651-480-9646.
summary: Hastings Family Service needs Saturday morning volunteers for their client-choice food market. Shifts run 8:30am–12:30pm. Ages 16+.

title: Interpretation: Somali & Hmong
description: Help community members navigate benefits applications and service intake. Flexible hours. Stipend available.

title: Mobile Pantry Drivers, Saturdays
description: Deliver food to mobile distribution sites across Dakota County. Valid license, ability to lift 40lbs. 2–3 hours. Mileage reimbursed.

title: Pro Bono Legal Help for Immigration Cases
description: Attorneys available for DACA renewals, asylum applications, and family petitions. By appointment through VLN intake.

title: Room Available in North Minneapolis, Low Cost
description: Furnished room in shared house, $400/mo utilities included. Near bus lines 5 and 22. Prefer someone in recovery or recently housed. References required.

title: Spring Toy Drive Starts Soon: Drop Off Donations
description:
The holiday season proved that families show up for kids when you make it easy. Now STEP is getting ahead of spring. Their annual spring toy and children's supply drive begins in March, collecting new toys, books, art supplies, and outdoor gear for kids in St. Louis Park and surrounding communities.

Drop off donations at **6812 W Lake St, St. Louis Park**, Tuesday and Thursday from 9am to noon, or Saturday mornings from 9am to 11am beginning March 8. New items only; no used toys, please. If you want to organize a drive at your workplace or school, contact STEP at **952-928-6082** and they will give you a collection bin and a flyer.
summary: STEP in St. Louis Park is collecting new toys and kids' supplies starting March 8. Drop off at 6812 W Lake St. Call 952-928-6082 to organize a drive.

title: Vietnamese Interpreters Needed for Health Appointments
description:
Vietnamese Social Services of Minnesota (VSSMN) is looking for bilingual Vietnamese-English volunteers to accompany community members to medical and dental appointments in the St. Paul area. Most appointments are weekday mornings.

Time commitment: 2–4 hours per month. You do not need formal interpreter training; VSSMN will provide orientation. Volunteers must be 18+, comfortable in clinical settings, and available for a brief intake call.

This is meaningful, hands-on work. Many VSSMN clients are elderly and navigate appointments alone. Your presence changes how those visits go.

Contact: Tuyen Nguyen, 651-472-7979 or info@vssmn.org.
summary: VSSMN needs bilingual Vietnamese-English volunteers to accompany elderly community members to medical appointments. 2–4 hours/month. Call 651-472-7979.

title: Volunteers Needed at Open Cupboard, Saturdays
description: Open Cupboard in Oakdale needs volunteers to help stock shelves, assist clients at intake, and bag groceries every Saturday 8:30 am–12:30 pm. No experience required. Minimum commitment is two Saturdays per month. Must be 16 or older. Email volunteer@opencupboard.org to sign up.
summary: Open Cupboard in Oakdale needs Saturday volunteers 8:30 am–12:30 pm. Stock shelves, assist clients. Must be 16+, two Saturdays/month minimum.

title: Weekday Volunteers Needed at Shakopee Food Shelf
description:
CAP Agency's Scott County food shelf needs 15 to 20 volunteers per week to keep up with growing demand. Shifts are Tuesday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to noon.

Tasks include unloading deliveries, sorting donations, stocking shelves, and welcoming clients. No experience required; training takes about 20 minutes. Background checks are not required for general food shelf shifts.

If you can commit to one shift per week, contact CAP directly to sign up. They especially need help on Tuesdays when the weekly Second Harvest delivery arrives.
summary: CAP Shakopee needs 15–20 volunteers per week, Tuesday–Friday mornings. No experience required. Especially need Tuesday help for Second Harvest deliveries.

title: Winter Coats & Boots, All Sizes
description: Accepting clean, gently used winter coats, boots, gloves, and hats. Children's sizes especially needed. Drop-off M–F 8am–6pm at front desk.

title: Belle Plaine Food Shelf Adds Saturday Hours Through March
description:
The Belle Plaine Food Shelf has extended its winter schedule to include Saturday morning service through March 28. Hours are now Monday and Wednesday 9 a.m.–noon, Thursday 5–7 p.m., and Saturday 9–11 a.m.

No appointment needed. No ID required. Residents from Belle Plaine and surrounding townships are welcome. The shelf stocks fresh produce, dairy, canned goods, and household staples. Items are distributed on a first-come, first-served basis.

Donations of non-perishable food and personal care items are accepted during all open hours.
summary: Belle Plaine Food Shelf has added Saturday 9–11 a.m. service through March 28. No appointment, no ID needed. Fresh produce and household staples available.

title: Boil Water Advisory: North Minneapolis
description: Water main break at 26th & Lyndale. Residents between 24th and 28th Streets should boil water before drinking until further notice. Free bottled water at Whittier Park.

title: Burnsville Pantry Lets You Choose Your Own Groceries
description:
The 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville runs a client-choice model, which means you walk through real aisles and pick what your family actually eats, not a box someone packed for you. Fresh vegetables, frozen proteins, canned goods, dairy, and pantry staples are available depending on donations that week.

Open Tuesday through Friday, 9am to 3pm at **14750 Cedar Ave S, Suite 100, Burnsville**. You can call ahead at **952-985-5300** or walk in during hours. Bring proof of Dakota County address for your first visit. After that, you are in the system and can return monthly. No income verification required.
summary: 360 Communities in Burnsville lets you pick your own groceries from real aisles. Open Tue–Fri 9am–3pm. Call 952-985-5300.
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf

title: Call Before Your Eviction Hearing. Free Help Available
description:
If you have received an eviction summons and your court date is coming up, do not wait until the morning of to figure out your options. HOME Line's tenant counselors will explain what happens at an eviction hearing, help you understand what defenses you may have, and tell you what to bring. They cannot represent you in court, but they can make sure you walk in prepared.

If your hearing is less than seven days away, say that when you call, and they will prioritize your case. The hotline is at **612-728-5767**, Monday through Friday, 9am to 4:30pm. Free. No ID required. Spanish and Somali interpretation available.
summary: Got an eviction summons? HOME Line will walk you through your options before court. Call 612-728-5767, free, no ID needed.

title: Emergency Cash Available: Apply at Scott County This Week
description:
Scott County Financial Assistance is accepting applications for Emergency General Assistance (EGA) and Crisis Funds through the end of February. EGA provides one-time cash grants to county residents facing sudden financial hardship: unpaid rent, utility shutoffs, or a medical bill that wiped out savings.

Eligibility: Scott County resident, U.S. citizen or eligible immigrant, income at or below 200% of federal poverty guidelines. No asset test.

Apply in person at the Human Services building in Shakopee. Bring proof of residency, income documentation, and a description of the crisis. Walk-ins welcome; call ahead for same-day appointments.
summary: Scott County is accepting Emergency General Assistance applications now through end of February. One-time cash grants for rent, utilities, or crisis expenses. Walk-ins welcome in Shakopee.

title: Emergency Heating Assistance: Apply Before March 14
description:
Community Action Partnership of Ramsey & Washington Counties (CAPRW) is accepting emergency heating assistance applications through March 14, 2026. Funds are limited and will close when exhausted.

Who qualifies: households at or below 50% of the state median income. Renters and homeowners are both eligible. Documentation of income and a recent utility bill required.

How to apply:
- Walk-in: 450 N. Syndicate St., St. Paul, Mon–Fri 8:30am–4:30pm
- By phone: 651-645-6806
- Online: caprw.org/energy-assistance

If your heat has already been shut off, call the emergency line at 651-645-6806 ext. 100; same-day assistance may be available.
summary: CAPRW is taking heating assistance applications through March 14. Funds will close early. Walk in to 450 N. Syndicate or call 651-645-6806.
source: Community Action Partnership of Ramsey & Washington Counties (CAPRW)

title: Emergency Rent Assistance Open: Apply This Week
description:
Ramsey County's emergency financial assistance program has rent funds available right now for residents facing eviction or who are behind on rent. If you have received a late notice or an eviction filing, you may qualify for help covering what you owe.

Applications are accepted at the Ramsey County Financial Assistance office. Bring your lease, the past-due notice or eviction summons, proof of income (a recent pay stub or benefits letter), and a photo ID. Funds are limited and distributed while they last, so don't wait until the deadline passes. Call **651-266-4444** to schedule an intake appointment. Walk-ins are accepted but appointments move faster. Interpretation available in Somali, Spanish, Hmong, and Karen.
summary: Ramsey County has emergency rent funds available now. Bring your lease and past-due notice. Call 651-266-4444 to schedule.

title: Emergency Rent Help Available: Apply Before March 31
description:
CAP Agency's Dakota County Office (14625 Galaxie Ave, Rosemount) has emergency rental assistance funds available for income-qualified residents facing eviction or housing instability. Applications accepted through March 31, 2026, while funds last.

Who qualifies: Dakota County residents with a documented housing crisis (eviction notice, past-due rent, loss of income). Bring a copy of your lease, the eviction notice or late notice, and a recent pay stub or benefits statement. Appointments required; call 651-322-3500 to schedule. Spanish and Somali interpretation available on request.
summary: CAP Agency has emergency rental assistance for Dakota County residents facing eviction. Funds are limited, so apply before March 31. Call 651-322-3500.

title: Food Shelf Hours Extended Through April
description: Friends In Need Food Shelf in Cottage Grove has added Saturday morning hours through April 26. The shelf is open Tuesdays 4–7 pm, Thursdays 9 am–noon, and now Saturdays 9 am–noon. No ID required. Clients may visit once per week. Call 651-458-1234 before your first visit.
summary: Friends In Need Food Shelf adds Saturday hours through April. Open Tue 4–7 pm, Thu 9 am–noon, Sat 9 am–noon. No ID required.

title: Free Tax Prep Sites Open at 12 Library Locations
description: VITA volunteers help with federal and state returns. Walk-ins Saturdays 10am–2pm. By appointment weekdays.

title: Hennepin County Opens 3 New Warming Centers Through March
description: Locations in Brooklyn Center, downtown Minneapolis, and Bloomington. Open 6pm–8am nightly. No ID required.

title: ICE Activity Reported Near Lake Street
description: Know your rights: you do not have to open your door. You have the right to remain silent. Call the hotline if you witness activity.

title: Know Your Rights if ICE Comes to Your Door
description:
The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota wants every family to know four things before opening their door:

1. You do not have to open the door for ICE unless they have a judicial warrant signed by a judge. An administrative warrant is not enough.
2. You have the right to remain silent. You do not have to answer questions about your immigration status.
3. Do not sign anything without speaking to a lawyer first.
4. Call the ILCM emergency line immediately: 612-358-0111.

ILCM is offering free Know Your Rights sessions every Saturday in March at their office at 450 N. Syndicate St., St. Paul (Room 200). Sessions run 10am–noon. No appointment needed. Spanish, Hmong, Somali, and Karen interpretation will be available on-site.
summary: ILCM has four rules for what to do if ICE comes to your door, plus free Saturday workshops through March at their St. Paul office.

title: Make Your Community Count
description: Every person counted in the census means more federal funding for schools, roads, and emergency services in Minnesota.

title: Metro Transit Adds Overnight Service on Route 5
description: Starting March 1, Route 5 will run hourly between midnight and 5am on weekends, connecting downtown Minneapolis to the airport.

title: Minneapolis Public Schools Announce Snow Day Protocol Changes
description: MPS will shift to e-learning days instead of traditional snow day cancellations starting with the next weather event.

title: MNsure Open Enrollment Extended to March 15
description: Minnesotans can still apply for health coverage. Free navigators available at mnsure.org or call 855-366-7873.

title: Neighbors Inc. Food Shelf Now Open Saturdays
description:
Starting March 7, Neighbors, Inc. (166 W. Geneva Ave, South St. Paul) will open its food shelf on Saturdays from 9am to noon. This adds a third distribution day alongside their regular Tuesday and Thursday hours (9am–noon and 5–7pm).

No appointment needed on Saturdays. Bring proof of address for Dakota County residents. The expanded hours are made possible by new volunteer capacity. If you want to help staff a Saturday shift, contact volunteer coordinator Jana Hoffman at 651-457-7305 ext. 2.
summary: Neighbors, Inc. food shelf in South St. Paul is adding Saturday morning hours starting March 7. Walk-ins welcome, 9am–noon.

title: Ramsey County Adds Walk-In Hours for Emergency Cash
description:
Ramsey County Human Services has added two evening walk-in windows for emergency financial assistance, effective March 2. If you are facing eviction, utility shutoff, or another housing crisis, you can now walk in without an appointment on Tuesday and Thursday evenings, 4–7pm, at 160 E. Kellogg Blvd., St. Paul.

Daytime walk-ins continue Mon–Fri 8am–4:30pm. Bring proof of income, ID if you have it (no ID required for all programs), and documentation of the crisis, such as an eviction notice, shutoff notice, or similar.

For questions: 651-266-4444.
summary: Ramsey County now offers Tuesday and Thursday evening walk-in hours for emergency financial help at 160 E. Kellogg Blvd. No appointment needed.

title: Register to Vote or Check Your Status
description: Minnesota allows same-day voter registration, but registering early saves time. Bring proof of residence.

title: SNAP Benefits: March EBT Deposits Begin March 4
description: Deposits will be staggered over 10 days. Check your deposit date at mn.gov/dhs/snap.

title: SNAP Benefits: Processing Delays Expected This Week
description: MN DHS reports processing delays affecting EBT deposits. Check mn.gov/dhs for updates.

title: Spring Fund Drive: Help Cover Summer Gaps
description: Basic Needs Inc. runs its annual spring fund drive through March 31. Donations cover emergency rent and utility assistance for South Washington County families who fall through the cracks between government programs. Online giving at basicneeds.org. Checks payable to Basic Needs Inc., PO Box 3, St. Paul Park, MN 55071.
summary: Basic Needs Inc. fund drive runs through March 31. Donations fill emergency assistance gaps for South Washington County families. Give online or by mail.

title: Tell Your Rep: Fund Emergency Rental Assistance
description: HF 1247 would allocate $50M for emergency rental assistance. The bill is in committee now. One phone call makes a difference.

title: Winter Shelter Beds Available Tonight, Walk-ins Welcome
description: Overflow shelters open at Simpson Housing (2740 1st Ave S) and Mary's Place (401 N 7th St). No ID required. Temps dropping to −8°F tonight. Metro Transit routes 5 and 14 stop within one block. Call 612-874-8683 for transport assistance.

title: Youth Shelters Have Beds: Spread the Word
description:
If you know a young person between 16 and 24 who is sleeping on couches, in cars, or outside, there are shelter beds available right now in Hennepin County. Simpson Housing Services runs transitional housing programs specifically for young adults, with case management, job readiness support, and help finding a permanent place to live.

The hardest part for most young people is making the first call. Many don't think of themselves as homeless; they're just "staying with a friend for a while." If you are an adult in a young person's life and you suspect they don't have a stable place to sleep, share this number: **612-874-8683**. Simpson's intake team can talk through options without pressure. Emergency drop-in at **2740 1st Ave S, Minneapolis**.
summary: Simpson Housing has transitional beds for young adults 16–24. If you know someone couch-surfing, share this number: 612-874-8683.

title: Dakota County Food Shelves: Locations and Hours
description: Community food shelves serving Dakota County residents. Most do not require proof of income or documentation. Call ahead to confirm hours before visiting, as schedules can change.
summary: Food shelves across Dakota County: addresses, hours, and phone numbers. Most locations require no ID or documentation.

title: Barb Johnson Stitches Washington County Together
description: Barb Johnson has run the volunteer program at Community Thread in Stillwater for eleven years. What started as a one-day-a-week commitment became a full calling. She coordinates more than 120 active volunteers who deliver meals, run the thrift store, lead craft groups, and staff the food shelf. "People come for a coat and leave with a friend," she says. Community Thread served over 6,000 Washington County residents last year.
summary: Eleven years in, Barb Johnson has made Community Thread a place where Washington County neighbors find more than just what they came for.

person.name: Barb Johnson
person.bio: Barb has coordinated over 120 active volunteers at Community Thread for eleven years, connecting Washington County residents with meals, clothing, and community.
person.quote: People come for a coat and leave with a friend.

title: Deb Hartmann Has Run New Prague's Food Shelf for 11 Years
description:
Deb Hartmann started volunteering at the Peace Center Food Shelf the year her youngest started high school. She became coordinator two years later and hasn't looked back.

Under her watch, the shelf grew from a twice-monthly distribution to a full weekly operation with a mobile pantry component. She added fresh produce sourced from area farms, secured a relationship with a regional food bank, and recruited a core of 30 regular volunteers.

"The hardest part isn't the logistics," she said. "It's getting people to walk through the door the first time. There's still so much shame around needing help. Our job is to make this feel like a normal place to be."

The Peace Center serves New Prague, Montgomery, Lonsdale, and surrounding townships. Deb trains all new volunteers herself.
summary: Deb Hartmann has run the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague for 11 years, growing it from a twice-monthly handout to a full weekly operation with a mobile pantry and 30 regular volunteers.

person.name: Deb Hartmann
person.bio: Deb has run the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague for 11 years, growing it from a twice-monthly distribution to a full weekly operation with a mobile pantry and 30 regular volunteers.
person.quote: The hardest part isn't the logistics. It's getting people to walk through the door the first time.

title: Destiny Beat the Odds. Now She's Serving Her Country
description:
Destiny does not talk about her childhood like it was a tragedy. She talks about it like a set of facts she had to work around. A mother whose mental health made home unpredictable. A junior year that started on a friend's couch in St. Paul after her mother left the state. Months of not knowing where she would sleep next week.

What she does talk about is what changed things: a youth shelter that gave her a stable address. A transitional housing program that let her stay enrolled in school. Adults who showed up consistently—caseworkers, teachers, a shelter volunteer who drove her to a college visit.

She graduated high school on time. Earned a degree from Spelman College cum laude. Completed law school in the Twin Cities and accepted a commission as a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force. Catholic Charities Twin Cities provided housing navigation during a critical stretch of her transition. Their intake line is **651-232-5428**.
summary: Destiny went from couch-surfing to the Air Force JAG Corps. Catholic Charities helped her find stable ground during the stretch that mattered most.

person.name: Destiny
person.bio: Destiny grew up in Twin Cities poverty, couch-surfed through high school, graduated on time, earned honors at Spelman College, and completed law school in 2025. She is now a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force.
person.quote: I didn't need someone to fix everything. I needed one person to say: you can do this.

title: Keystone's Pantry Manager Has Fed This Block for 14 Years
description:
Marcus Osei started volunteering at Keystone Community Services' Community Food Center the year he moved to St. Paul. That was 2012. Today he manages the pantry full-time, overseeing a team of 30 volunteers who keep the shelves stocked at 1090 University Ave. W.

The Keystone food center serves roughly 600 households a month, operating Monday through Friday, 9am–4pm. Unlike many food shelves, Keystone uses a client-choice model where visitors walk the aisles and select what they want, like a small grocery store. Osei pushed hard for the switch eight years ago.

"People have allergies. People have preferences. People have dignity," he says. "You don't hand someone a random box and call it help."

Keystone also runs a mobile pantry that visits four apartment complexes in the Midway neighborhood on alternating Tuesdays, reaching residents who cannot easily travel to the main site.
summary: Marcus Osei has run Keystone's Community Food Center since 2012. He switched the pantry to a client-choice model because people deserve to pick their own food.

title: Margaret Tells Other Seniors: Don't Wait Like I Did
description:
Margaret was a medical records technician for most of her career: organized, private, and proud of not needing help. When she started visiting the 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville after retirement, it took her three months to stop apologizing to the volunteers at the door. Now she tells other seniors in her building not to wait the way she did.

She visits once a month, picks her own groceries from a real aisle layout, and makes casseroles that carry her through the week. She has started bringing a neighbor who lives on a similar budget but hadn't worked up the nerve to go alone. "I tell her it's groceries, not charity," Margaret says. "You walk in, you fill your cart, and nobody makes you feel small."

360 Communities Burnsville is at **14750 Cedar Ave S, Suite 100**. Food shelf hours: Tuesday through Friday, 9am to 3pm. Call **952-985-5300**.
summary: Margaret visits 360 Communities' Burnsville food shelf monthly and now brings a neighbor who was too proud to go alone.
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf

title: Rosa Delgado Has Worked the Same Food Shelf for 14 Years
description:
Rosa Delgado started as a Neighbors, Inc. volunteer in 2012. She didn't plan to stay. Now she's the food shelf coordinator at their South St. Paul site at 166 W. Geneva Ave, and she knows roughly half the families by name.

"The hardest part isn't the work," she says. "It's when families are embarrassed to come back a second time. I always tell them: we stocked these shelves for you. That's the whole point."

Under Rosa's coordination, Neighbors, Inc. expanded from two to three distribution days and launched a standing produce partnership with local grocery stores. She connects first-time visitors to Dakota County emergency assistance, utility help, and affordable housing resources when food insecurity is a symptom of a deeper crisis.

Neighbors, Inc. serves northern Dakota County including South St. Paul, Inver Grove Heights, Mendota Heights, and Sunfish Lake.
summary: Rosa Delgado has coordinated the Neighbors, Inc. food shelf in South St. Paul for 14 years. She knows half the families by name, and she wants them to come back.

title: She's Fed Thousands Without Ever Asking for a Card
description:
Fran White Eagle has been coordinating the Native American Food Shelf operated through Interfaith Action of Greater Saint Paul for eight years. Located at 1671 Summit Ave. in the lower level of the building, the food shelf is open Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10am–2pm, and has never required an ID, a tribal enrollment card, or a referral.

"People come in and we treat them like family, because they are family," White Eagle says. "Lakota, Ojibwe, Dakota, urban Indians, people who don't know their tribe. Everyone is welcome here."

The food shelf operates on donated food from Second Harvest Heartland and community drives, supplemented by a large garden maintained by volunteers every summer. White Eagle has expanded the pantry's offerings to include traditional foods (wild rice, fry bread mix, dried berries) alongside the standard staples.

Last year, the food shelf served over 4,200 households. White Eagle wants that number to grow.
summary: Fran White Eagle runs the Native American Food Shelf at Interfaith Action. No ID, no card, no questions. She's served 4,200 households and counting.
source: Interfaith Action of Greater Saint Paul (Native American Food Shelf)

person.quote: People come in and we treat them like family, because they are family.

title: St. Vinny's on Payne Ave: Thrift Store With a Mission
description:
The St. Vincent de Paul thrift store at 789 Payne Ave., St. Paul has been quietly doing two things at once for decades: selling donated goods at prices working families can afford, and using every dollar to fund emergency assistance for neighbors in crisis.

The St. Paul store is one of 11 operated by SVdP Twin Cities, and it is one of the busiest. On any given Saturday morning, the parking lot fills before 9am. Inside, shoppers sort through clothing, housewares, furniture, and electronics. Prices are kept deliberately low: a winter coat for $4.99, a working microwave for $12.

What many shoppers don't realize is that every dollar spent here funds the SVdP assistance program, which provides emergency help for rent, utilities, food, and medicine to anyone who walks into the store and asks. No application required. Neighbors helping neighbors.
summary: St. Vinny's on Payne Ave keeps prices low on purpose. Every dollar goes to emergency assistance for neighbors who walk in and ask. No application required.
source: St. Vincent de Paul – Twin Cities (St. Paul)

title: Theo Bauer Has Rung the Bell Three Years Running
description:
Theo Bauer is not the kind of person who joins committees or wears volunteer T-shirts. He is a retired electrician from Roseville who started ringing bells at the Salvation Army kettle outside a Target in 2023 because his wife signed them both up and he didn't have a good excuse. He has not missed a season since.

He rings two-hour shifts, usually Saturday afternoons, and estimates he has logged around 40 hours across three holiday seasons. He says the best part is not the ringing; it's the conversations. People stop and tell him about their year. Sometimes they tell him things they probably don't tell anyone else. "You'd be surprised how many people just want someone to say hello," Theo says.

The Salvation Army recruits bell ringers every November. No training required, no long-term commitment. Just show up. Sign up at **1000 Penn Ave N, North Minneapolis** or call **612-521-8742**.
summary: Theo Bauer has rung bells at a Roseville Target for three holiday seasons. He keeps coming back for the conversations, not the ringing.
source: The Salvation Army – North Minneapolis (Parkview Worship & Service Center)

title: 11,500 Bell Ringers Braved the Cold This Christmas
description:
Across Minnesota, more than eleven thousand volunteers picked up a bell and stood outside a store this holiday season. They logged over 26,000 hours between them, in Roseville, Duluth, Rochester, and across the Twin Cities metro. By nearly every measure, it was one of the strongest volunteer turnouts in recent memory.

What makes bell-ringing work is not complexity. There is no training, no background check, no long-term commitment. You show up, ring a bell for an hour or two, and go home. The Salvation Army uses those donations to fund food shelves, utility assistance, youth programs, and transitional housing year-round. The Parkview Center in North Minneapolis alone serves hundreds of families each month with services most people never associate with a red kettle.

The Salvation Army's Parkview Worship and Service Center is at **1000 Penn Ave N, North Minneapolis**. Walk-in services Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, 9am to noon. Call **612-521-8742**.
summary: More than 11,500 volunteers rang bells across Minnesota this Christmas, logging 26,000 hours. One of the strongest turnouts in years.
source: The Salvation Army – North Minneapolis (Parkview Worship & Service Center)

title: 14,000 Kids Got Gifts Because Strangers Showed Up
description:
Nearly fourteen thousand children across the Twin Cities received donated toys this past Christmas, and the way it happened matters. Instead of handing out whatever was available, organizers set up client-choice toy shops where parents could walk through and pick the gifts themselves, choosing what their kid would actually love, wrapping it at home, and putting their own name on the tag.

That distinction changes everything. A parent who gets to choose is not receiving charity. They are giving a gift. Community Action Partnership of Hennepin County coordinated several of these distribution sites, working with dozens of toy drive donors, corporate partners, and volunteer sorters to keep the shelves stocked and the lines moving.

CAP-HC serves Hennepin County residents with emergency assistance, housing navigation, and family support programs year-round. Main office: **7051 Brooklyn Blvd, Brooklyn Center**. Call **952-933-9639**.
summary: Nearly 14,000 Twin Cities kids received holiday gifts through client-choice toy shops that let parents pick and wrap the presents themselves.
source: Community Action Partnership of Hennepin County (CAP-HC)

title: Eviction Notices Are Piling Up. Here's What's Happening
description:
Minnesota courts processed more eviction filings last year than at any point since tracking began. The numbers nearly doubled from the pre-pandemic average, and the pace isn't slowing down. Hennepin and Ramsey counties account for the largest share, but suburban courts in Dakota, Scott, and Anoka are seeing sharp increases too. Vacancy rates across the Twin Cities have dropped below three percent, which means families who lose a unit have almost nowhere to go.

HOME Line, the statewide tenant hotline, has been fielding a rising volume of calls from renters who are behind on rent, confused by court notices, or unsure whether their landlord can legally lock them out. Many of the callers have never been through an eviction before. Their tenant counselors explain your rights, help you draft a response to your landlord, and connect you with legal aid when representation is needed. The call is free, and you don't need an ID. Dial **612-728-5767** Monday through Friday, 9am to 4:30pm.
summary: Minnesota eviction filings nearly doubled in one year. HOME Line's free tenant hotline at 612-728-5767 walks renters through their rights before court.

title: Family Kept the Lights On. Here's How
description:
Maria Reyes hadn't missed a bill in eleven years. Then a car repair wiped out her savings the same week her hours got cut at work. By the time she called 360 Communities at their Burnsville Resource Center (14750 Cedar Ave, Suite 100), she was ten days from a utility shutoff with two kids at home.

The intake coordinator sat with her for 90 minutes. They filed an emergency utility assistance application, connected her with a food shelf appointment for the following morning, and identified a rental assistance program she hadn't known existed. "I came in sure they couldn't help me," Maria said. "I left with a plan."

360 Communities serves residents of Burnsville, Apple Valley, Eagan, Lakeville, and surrounding communities. Their resource center offers food shelf access, financial coaching, and crisis stabilization, all under one roof. Call 952-985-5300 to schedule.
summary: A utility shutoff, two kids, and ten days to fix it. Maria Reyes walked into 360 Communities not knowing what to expect. She left with a plan.
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf

title: Food Shelf Lines Have Doubled Since 2022
description:
Three years ago, most metro food shelves served a predictable roster of regulars. That has changed. Monthly client counts at pantries across Hennepin County have roughly doubled since 2022, and the new visitors look different from who used to walk in. Families with two working parents. Seniors whose fixed incomes stopped covering groceries when prices climbed. People who describe themselves as embarrassed to be asking.

One in four Minnesota families now relies on a food shelf at some point during the year. The Food Group, which operates the statewide Food HelpLine and the Fare For All discount grocery program, has been coordinating supply across dozens of partner pantries to keep shelves stocked. If you need food, call the Food HelpLine at **888-711-1151** (Monday through Friday, 8am to 4:30pm) and they will connect you with the nearest pantry that has capacity. No income verification. No ID required at most locations.
summary: Monthly food shelf visits across the metro have doubled since 2022. The Food Group's HelpLine at 888-711-1151 connects you with the nearest pantry.
source: The Food Group (Food HelpLine / Fare For All / food bank)

title: From Couch-Surfing to the Courtroom: One Teen's Journey
description:
Destiny grew up in the Twin Cities with a mother whose mental illness made stability rare. When her mother left for the West Coast during Destiny's junior year of high school, she was on her own. She couch-surfed through St. Paul for months—sleeping at friends' apartments, eating what was available, trying to keep her grades up while having no fixed address.

A youth shelter connected her with transitional housing, and from that stable ground she did something remarkable: she graduated high school on time. Then Spelman College, cum laude. Then law school in the Twin Cities, where she earned her JD in 2025 and accepted a position as a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force.

Destiny's story is not about one program. It is about what happens when a young person finds a bed, a meal, and one adult who says "you can do this." People Serving People, the family shelter at **614 S 3rd St, Minneapolis**, provides exactly that for over 100 families every night. If you know a young person who needs shelter, call **612-332-4500**.
summary: Destiny went from couch-surfing in St. Paul to earning a law degree and a JAG commission. It started with a shelter bed and one person who believed her.

title: Halal Food Shelf Fills a Gap No One Else Would
description:
When Fadumo Osman moved her family to Burnsville from Minneapolis four years ago, the nearest halal food options were a 40-minute round trip. Generic food shelves helped with pantry staples but couldn't provide the meat and culturally appropriate ingredients her family actually cooked with. She heard about Isuroon from a neighbor.

Isuroon's Burnsville office at 300 E Travelers Trail operates a halal food shelf serving Somali and East African families across the south metro. Beyond groceries, they offer navigation help for benefits, housing referrals, and connections to Somali-speaking case workers who can cut through bureaucratic confusion. "They understand why I'm here and what I actually need," Fadumo said. "That's rare."

The food shelf runs Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10am–2pm. No appointment required. For more information call 952-303-3489.
summary: Generic food shelves couldn't stock what Fadumo Osman's family actually cooks. Isuroon's Burnsville halal food shelf changed that.

title: How a Block Club in Phillips Organized Its Own Warming Station
description: When the city couldn't keep up, neighbors on 25th & Bloomington opened their church basement.

title: How CLUES Became St. Paul's Living Room
description:
On any given Tuesday morning, the lobby at CLUES (Comunidades Latinas Unidas En Servicio) on Minnehaha Avenue looks less like a nonprofit waiting room and more like a reunion. An older woman finishes filling out her food shelf application while chatting with the intake worker in Spanish. Two teenagers review college application essays at a corner table. A father who arrived from Oaxaca three months ago waits for his appointment with an employment counselor.

CLUES has been at this corner of St. Paul since 1981, growing from a small advocacy office into a hub that now serves more than 8,000 individuals a year. The range is remarkable: workforce training, mental health counseling, food access, senior services, citizenship classes, and a thriving arts and culture program. What binds it together is the language: staff communicate in Spanish, English, and Somali, and interpretation is always available.

"People come here because they know they won't have to explain themselves," says Ana Fuentes, a case manager who has worked at CLUES for eleven years. "They can just ask for what they need."

The organization recently opened a second site in Roseville to extend services to the growing Latino population in the northern suburbs, a move that reflects just how much the Twin Cities community has changed since CLUES first opened its doors four decades ago.
summary: CLUES on Minnehaha Avenue serves 8,000 people a year, not by specializing, but by staying put and staying open to whatever neighbors bring through the door.
source: CLUES (Comunidades Latinas Unidas En Servicio) – St. Paul

title: "I Didn't Know Where to Turn": How One Family Found Help After a House Fire
description: When a kitchen fire displaced the Nguyen family from their Brooklyn Center apartment in January, they had no renters insurance and no relatives nearby. With two school-age children and a newborn, Linh Nguyen spent the first night at a hotel paid for by the Red Cross, unsure what would happen next. A case worker at Hennepin County connected the family with CEAP, the Community Emergency Assistance Programs, which has been serving northwest-metro residents since 1970. Within 48 hours, CEAP helped the Nguyens secure temporary housing, provided furniture and household essentials, and connected Linh with a benefits navigator. "They didn't just give us a list of phone numbers," Linh said through an interpreter. "They walked with us through every step. They showed up."
summary: After a kitchen fire left them homeless, the Nguyen family found a community that showed up, not with a list of numbers, but with real help.

title: Minnesota Resettles 1,200 Afghan Refugees: What's Next
description: A look at what comes after arrival: housing, employment, language access, and community integration for newly arrived Afghan families.

title: Point-in-Time Count Shows 12% Drop in Unsheltered Homelessness
description: The latest Wilder Foundation count shows a meaningful decline in unsheltered homelessness across the Twin Cities metro.

title: Shakopee's Quiet Hunger Problem
description:
The CAP Agency food shelf on Vierling Drive doesn't look like a place where 400 families a month come in for help. The waiting room is calm. Staff greet people by name. Kids do homework at a corner table while a parent fills out intake paperwork.

But the numbers are stark. Last year, CAP Scott County distributed over 200,000 pounds of food, up 18 percent from the year before. Most clients are working. Many live in the newer subdivisions along the 169 corridor, places that don't look like they have a food problem from the outside.

"People drive nice cars and still can't make ends meet at the end of the month," said the food shelf coordinator. "The cost of housing out here has outpaced wages. That gap is where we live."

CAP accepts walk-ins with no ID required. Families can visit once per month and choose their own groceries from a client-choice model, not pre-boxed bags. The agency also connects clients to utility assistance, eviction prevention, and job training on-site.

Volunteers are the backbone. CAP needs 15 to 20 people per week to sort and stock shelves, greet clients, and help with mobile pantry deliveries to Jordan and Prior Lake. Shifts run Tuesday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to noon.
summary: CAP Agency's Shakopee food shelf served 400 families last month, most of them working. A rising cost-of-living gap is driving demand up 18 percent year over year.

title: She Worked Her Whole Life. Now She Eats Twice a Day
description:
Margaret spent decades working in medical records at a hospital south of the metro. She retired at 72 because her body told her to, not because the math worked out. Her Social Security check barely covers rent and utilities in her Burnsville apartment, so she made adjustments. Two meals a day instead of three. Driving only when absolutely necessary. Skipping the things most people don't think twice about.

Once a month, she drives four miles to the 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville, where she fills a cart with vegetables, frozen proteins, and canned goods, her pick, not a pre-packed box. She makes casseroles that stretch across the week. She is deeply grateful to the volunteers who stock those shelves and the donors who fund them. But she will also tell you, quietly, that she never expected to be here.

360 Communities' Burnsville Resource Center is at **14750 Cedar Ave S, Suite 100**. The food shelf is open Tuesday through Friday, 9am to 3pm. Call **952-985-5300** to schedule or walk in during hours.
summary: Margaret retired after decades of work. Her Social Security doesn't cover groceries. The 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville lets her choose what she needs.
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf

title: Thousands Expected at Region's Largest Winter Food Drive
description: Over 40 organizations converge on the Minneapolis Convention Center for the annual Fill the Shelves drive.

title: Twin Cities Urban Tree Canopy Drops 8% Since 2015
description: A new MN DNR report documents the decline of tree cover across Minneapolis and St. Paul, with impacts concentrated in lower-income neighborhoods.

title: Valley Outreach Kept the Lights On
description: Last February, Denise Halverson got a shutoff notice three days after her husband was laid off. She had never asked for help before. A neighbor told her about Valley Outreach in Stillwater, and she drove over that afternoon. Within two hours she had a utility assistance check and a bag of groceries. Six months later she is a regular volunteer, sorting produce on Thursday mornings. "I wanted to give back before I even stopped needing help," she said. Valley Outreach served more than 4,200 households in Washington County last year, distributing over 700,000 pounds of food and connecting families with emergency financial assistance for rent, utilities, and medical bills.
summary: After a layoff pushed her family to the edge, Denise Halverson found help, and a new purpose, at Valley Outreach. Her story is one of thousands from Washington County last year.

title: What Karen Families Found When They Stayed
description:
When Paw Htoo arrived in St. Paul in 2009, she spoke no English and knew no one outside her resettlement cohort. Her caseworker at the time handed her a bus map and a list of appointments. She made every one of them, and then she kept showing up, long after the requirements ended, to help the families who arrived after her.

Seventeen years later, Htoo works as a community health navigator for the Karen Organization of Minnesota, which is based on University Avenue and serves roughly 2,500 Karen and Karenni community members in the metro. The organization began as an informal meeting group in the early 2000s and has grown into a full social services agency offering English classes, employment support, health navigation, cultural programming, and emergency financial assistance.

What keeps families here, Htoo says, is not just services; it is people. "When your neighbor speaks your language and knows what the first winter felt like, you stay."

KOM's health navigation program, funded in part through a partnership with Regions Hospital, has become a model studied by resettlement organizations in other states. Community members serve as frontline navigators, translating not just language but context, helping families understand how the U.S. healthcare system works and what questions to ask.
summary: The Karen Organization of Minnesota grew from a meeting group into a full social services agency. Its secret: neighbors navigating for neighbors.

title: Groceries and a Warm Welcome in Cottage Grove
description: Friends In Need Food Shelf has served the Cottage Grove area for over thirty years, and the doors are still open every week. Walk in Tuesday or Thursday morning and you will find shelves stocked with fresh produce, dairy, meat, and pantry basics. Volunteers greet every visitor by name when they can. There is no paperwork the first time you come. If you live in South Washington County and need a hand with groceries, this is the place to start.
summary: Friends In Need in Cottage Grove is open Tue and Thu mornings with fresh produce, dairy, and staples. No paperwork your first visit.
source: Friends In Need Food Shelf (Cottage Grove)

title: Pick Up Free Groceries in Oakdale Every Wednesday
description: Open Cupboard runs a client-choice food shelf out of its Oakdale location every Wednesday afternoon. You walk the aisles and pick what your family actually eats. Produce, frozen protein, bread, and personal care items are usually in stock. No appointment and no ID required on your first visit. Volunteers can help carry bags to your car. The shelf serves anyone living in the Oakdale, Lake Elmo, and Landfall area.
summary: Open Cupboard in Oakdale is open Wednesdays. Walk the aisles, choose what you need. No appointment or ID on your first visit.

title: Get Help with Groceries in South Washington County
description: Basic Needs Inc. stocks its shelves with fresh and shelf-stable food for families across South Washington County. The food shelf is open multiple days each week and there is no long intake process. Bring a piece of mail showing your address and you are good to go. Volunteers bag groceries, help carry, and point you toward other county resources if you need them. If your budget is stretched thin this month, this is the low-pressure place to start.
summary: Basic Needs Inc. provides groceries to South Washington County families multiple days a week. Bring proof of address, no lengthy intake.

title: Eden Prairie Families Can Get Emergency Help Fast
description: PROP helps Eden Prairie and Chanhassen residents who are one bill away from a crisis. If you are behind on rent, facing a utility shutoff, or need groceries to get through the week, PROP's team can usually start the process the same day you call. The food shelf is stocked with fresh and frozen options and you pick what works for your household. Financial assistance covers rent deposits, utility payments, and emergency prescriptions. Everything stays confidential.
summary: PROP in Eden Prairie offers same-day help with rent, utilities, and groceries. You choose your own food, everything stays confidential.
source: PROP (People Reaching Out to People) – Eden Prairie

title: One Phone Call Changed Everything for a Shakopee Family
description: When the heating bill hit three months overdue last January, a Shakopee father of two picked up the phone and called CAP Agency's Scott County office. Within a week he had an energy assistance application in process, a referral to the food shelf downstairs, and a budget coach walking him through next steps. Six months later the family is current on utilities and building a small savings cushion. The food shelf filled the gap while every dollar went toward the bill. That is how the CAP Agency office in Shakopee works: one call connects you to several kinds of help at once.
summary: A Shakopee dad called CAP Agency about a heating bill and left with energy aid, groceries, and a budget plan. His family is back on track.
source: CAP Agency – Scott County Office / Food Shelf (Shakopee)

title: Brooklyn Park Program Helped 200 Immigrants Land Jobs
description: CAPI USA's employment team in Brooklyn Park placed more than 200 job seekers into stable positions last year, most of them recent immigrants and refugees. The program pairs each participant with a bilingual job coach who helps with applications, interview practice, and workplace culture questions that never come up in a job listing. Many participants arrive speaking limited English and leave with a paycheck and growing confidence. CAPI also runs English classes, a food shelf, and financial coaching under one roof, so people can handle several needs in a single visit.
summary: CAPI USA in Brooklyn Park placed over 200 immigrants into jobs last year through bilingual coaching, English classes, and on-site support.
source: CAPI USA (Communities Advancing Prosperity for Immigrants)

title: Cedar-Riverside Food Shelf Now Open Four Days a Week
description: Pillsbury United Communities has expanded food shelf hours at the Brian Coyle Center in the Cedar-Riverside neighborhood. The shelf is now open Monday through Thursday from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m., up from two days a week last year. Visitors choose their own groceries from a selection that includes halal meat, fresh vegetables, rice, and cooking oils. No ID is needed on your first visit. The center also runs youth programs and community space, so families can handle multiple errands in one stop.
summary: Brian Coyle Center food shelf in Cedar-Riverside is now open Mon-Thu, 10am-2pm. Client choice, halal options, no ID first visit.
source: Pillsbury United Communities – Brian Coyle Center (Food Shelf)

title: Scott County Emergency Funds Helped 1,400 Families
description: When a car breaks down or a medical bill lands in the same month as rent, Scott County's emergency financial assistance program can step in fast. Last year the program helped more than 1,400 households keep the lights on, cover a security deposit, or fill a prescription they could not afford. Applications go through Scott County Human Services and most decisions come within a few business days. You do not need to be on any other program to qualify. Bring proof of income, a lease or mortgage statement, and an ID. Staff can walk you through the rest.
summary: Scott County emergency funds helped 1,400 families last year with rent, utilities, and prescriptions. Apply through Human Services, decisions in days.
source: Scott County Financial Assistance (Crisis Funds / Emergency General Assistance)

title: Volunteers Needed to Sort Donations in Golden Valley
description: PRISM's Golden Valley food shelf is looking for weekday morning volunteers to sort incoming donations, stock shelves, and help families carry groceries to their cars. Shifts run 9 a.m. to noon, Tuesday through Friday. No experience needed. You will be paired with a regular volunteer your first day. PRISM serves more than 4,000 households a year from western Hennepin County and the volume keeps climbing. Even one morning a week makes a real difference.
summary: PRISM in Golden Valley needs morning volunteers Tue-Fri to sort food and help families. No experience required, shifts 9am-noon.

title: ICA Needs Drivers to Deliver Groceries Across Minneapolis
description: ICA Food Shelf is looking for volunteer drivers to deliver grocery boxes to homebound seniors and families without reliable transportation across Minneapolis. Routes run on Wednesday and Saturday mornings and take about two hours. You use your own vehicle. ICA provides the boxes, the addresses, and a map. A valid license and proof of insurance are all you need to get started. Demand for home delivery has tripled since last year.
summary: ICA Food Shelf needs volunteer drivers on Wednesdays and Saturdays to deliver groceries across Minneapolis. Two-hour routes, your own car.

title: South Minneapolis Center Looking for After-School Tutors
description: Sabathani Community Center in South Minneapolis needs volunteer tutors for its after-school homework help program. Students range from first through eighth grade. Tutors come one or two afternoons a week, 3:30 to 5:30 p.m., Monday through Thursday. No teaching background required. Staff provide materials and a quiet room. A background check is needed before you start. The program serves about 40 kids each week and the waitlist is growing.
summary: Sabathani needs after-school tutors Mon-Thu, 3:30-5:30pm for grades 1-8. No teaching experience needed, background check required.

title: Bloomington Food Shelf Hosting Spring Restock Drive March 21
description: VEAP's Bloomington location is hosting a spring restock drive on Saturday, March 21, from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m. on Old Shakopee Road. Drop off canned protein, cooking oil, rice, pasta, diapers, and personal care items. Financial donations are welcome too. Volunteers will be on hand to unload your car. VEAP serves more than 10,000 people a year across Bloomington, Richfield, and Edina, and spring is when shelves run thinnest after the holiday donation season fades.
summary: VEAP hosts a spring food drive in Bloomington on March 21, 9am-1pm. Drop off pantry staples, diapers, and personal care items.

title: Free Immigration Legal Clinic in St. Paul March 18
description: The Advocates for Human Rights is holding a free immigration legal clinic on Wednesday, March 18, from 5 to 8 p.m. at a community site in St. Paul. Volunteer attorneys will offer one-on-one consultations on asylum cases, DACA renewals, family petitions, and other immigration questions. Interpreters will be available for Spanish, Somali, and Oromo. No appointment necessary, but arriving early is recommended since slots fill quickly. Bring any paperwork you have, including court notices, work permits, and ID. The clinic is open to anyone regardless of status.
summary: Free immigration legal clinic March 18, 5-8pm in St. Paul. Volunteer attorneys cover asylum, DACA, family petitions. Interpreters available.
source: The Advocates for Human Rights (Immigrant Client Line)

title: JFCS Open House Connects Families to Mental Health Support
description: Jewish Family and Children's Service of Minnesota is hosting an open house on Saturday, March 14, from 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. at its St. Louis Park office. Counselors and case managers will be on hand to answer questions about therapy, senior care, refugee resettlement, and financial coaching. There is no cost to attend and no obligation to sign up for anything. The goal is to help people learn what is available before a crisis hits. Light refreshments served. Parking is free.
summary: JFCS open house March 14, 10am-1pm in St. Louis Park. Meet counselors, learn about therapy, senior care, refugee services. Free, no strings.
source: Jewish Family and Children's Service of Minnesota (JFCS)

title: Teresa Lopez Builds Careers One Resume at a Time
description: Teresa Lopez has spent six years at CLUES helping Latino job seekers in Minneapolis find work that fits their skills. She came to Minnesota from Guatemala at nineteen, landed her first job through a workforce program, and decided to pay it forward. Today she coaches about 80 people a year through resume writing, mock interviews, and employer introductions. Many of her clients arrive nervous and leave with a plan. The CareerForce Center at CLUES is one of the few places in the metro where the entire process happens in Spanish.
summary: Teresa Lopez coaches about 80 Latino job seekers a year at CLUES in Minneapolis, guiding them from first resume to first paycheck.

person.name: Teresa Lopez
person.bio: Teresa came to Minnesota from Guatemala at nineteen and found her footing through a workforce program. She has spent six years at CLUES helping Latino job seekers build resumes and land stable work.
person.quote: A good job changes everything. Not just the money. The way you carry yourself.

title: Deb Hartmann Turned a Closet into a Community Lifeline
description: Eleven years ago the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague fit inside a single closet at the back of a church. Deb Hartmann took it over and built it into a weekly operation with thirty volunteers, a mobile pantry that reaches rural Scott County, and a walk-in shelf stocked with fresh produce, dairy, and meat. She knows most visitors by name and makes a point of asking how the kids are doing. The food shelf now serves around 200 households a month, many of them families who drive twenty minutes or more from the surrounding countryside.
summary: Deb Hartmann grew the Peace Center Food Shelf from a church closet to a weekly operation serving 200 households across rural Scott County.

person.name: Deb Hartmann
person.bio: Deb has run the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague for eleven years, growing it from a closet-sized pantry to a full weekly operation with a mobile route and thirty regular volunteers.
person.quote: The hardest part is getting someone to walk through the door the first time. After that, they know they belong.
